title: The Sun Sets for Chinese Solar in the U.S. - The Wire China
gist: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act's tightened foreign-entity tax rules and a Pentagon blacklist are forcing Chinese solar giants (JA Solar, Longi, Trina, Jinko) to sell down U.S. stakes below 25% ownership, even as China retains structural dominance across the global solar supply chain.

insights.claim: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed into law July 4, 2025, tightened tax-incentive eligibility rules and reversed Biden-era policies that had created favorable terms for Chinese green investment in the U.S., specifically barring credits for firms with at least 25 percent Chinese ownership ('foreign entities of concern').
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Any Chinese-linked solar or green-tech investment in the U.S. tied to federal tax credits now faces a hard 25% ownership ceiling, forcing forced divestitures or restructurings.
insights.locator: Chinese solar companies are reducing their shares of their U.S. assets

insights.claim: By March 2026, Chinese companies had canceled or paused more than half of the over $14 billion they had pledged for U.S.-based green technology projects between 2022 and 2025, according to Rhodium Group's China Cross-Border Monitor.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A majority of announced Chinese green-tech capex in the U.S. is being unwound, a leading indicator for further asset sales and plant closures.
insights.locator: By March of this year, Chinese companies had canceled or paused

insights.claim: The Department of Defense added Trina Solar and JA Solar to its 1260H blacklist of companies with links to the Chinese military, as of the list's June 8, 2026 update.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A DoD blacklisting raises procurement, reputational, and partnership risk for any U.S. entity still doing business with these firms, accelerating decoupling.
insights.locator: last month the Department of Defense added two of China's leading solar firms

themes.theme: Ownership Restructuring by Chinese Solar Firms

insights.claim: JinkoSolar cut its stake in its Florida-based U.S. subsidiary from 100 percent to 24.9 percent in May 2026, selling the difference to FH Capital for $191.5 million, to stay under the OBBB's foreign-entity-of-concern ownership threshold.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Expect more Chinese solar manufacturers to sell down to just below 25% ownership in U.S. subsidiaries rather than exit outright, preserving some economic exposure while retaining tax-credit eligibility for the U.S. buyer.
insights.locator: JinkoSolar reduced its stake in its Florida-based subsidiary

insights.claim: Trina Solar sold its Texas manufacturing facility to U.S. firm T1 Energy in December 2024, and further cut its remaining equity stake to 11 percent by May 2026, having never held more than the 25 percent limit.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: T1 Energy is becoming a consolidation vehicle for former Chinese-linked U.S. solar assets, a theme to watch for further roll-up M&A.
insights.locator: Trina Solar sold its Texan facility to U.S. firm T1 Energy

insights.claim: New York-headquartered Corning, which is building a domestic solar supply chain, acquired JA Solar's Arizona-based solar production facility in 2025.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Non-Chinese industrials like Corning are directly absorbing divested Chinese solar manufacturing capacity, a tangible beneficiary of the decoupling trend.
insights.locator: New York-headquartered Corning, which has committed

insights.claim: Longi's U.S. joint-venture partners have taken control: NeoVolta raised its stake in their Georgia battery plant JV from 60 to 80 percent in April 2026, and Invenergy now holds a supermajority of their Ohio solar JV and has announced plans to remove China from its supply chain.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: U.S. JV partners are actively buying out Chinese co-investors and pledging supply-chain de-Sinicization, signaling durable structural change rather than a temporary compliance workaround.
insights.locator: Longi has also reduced its involvement in its joint ventures

themes.theme: Structural Chokepoints and China's Manufacturing Edge

insights.claim: Despite the divestments, U.S. solar companies remain dependent on China for specialized upstream components like solar cells and wafers, where China exercises continued dominance.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Ownership restructuring at the assembly level doesn't remove China from the U.S. solar value chain; the real bottleneck (cells/wafers) is untouched, capping how much 'reshoring' has actually occurred.
insights.locator: U.S. solar companies still face a chokepoint

insights.claim: Rhodium Group's Armand Meyer argues China's manufacturing edge in clean tech isn't purely technological — it is genuinely cheaper and more efficient to manufacture solar equipment in China, reducing Chinese firms' incentive to invest in higher-value overseas production.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Cost-driven (not just policy-driven) advantages mean Chinese solar dominance is likely to persist even if U.S. policy further restricts market access.
insights.quote: China still has the most competitive manufacturing ecosystem in the world, especially in clean tech. They don't just have a technological edge, but it's cheaper and more efficient to manufacture in China.
insights.locator: China still has the most competitive manufacturing ecosystem

insights.claim: China's solar cost collapse has been dramatic: in 2009, when China ranked eighth globally in solar capacity, producing one megawatt-hour of solar energy cost $496 versus $153 for coal; by 2024, with China holding almost five times the solar capacity of the U.S., solar generation cost had fallen to just $60/MWh.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: This cost trajectory is the fundamental economic force U.S. reshoring policy is fighting against — tariffs and ownership caps address market access, not the underlying cost gap.
insights.locator: In 2009, when China was a distant eighth place

insights.claim: China holds the largest global manufacturing share at every stage of the solar supply chain, from polysilicon to module assembly, and the IEA does not expect this dominance to wane soon.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Any U.S. 'de-risking' strategy must plan for a multi-decade timeline to build independent upstream capacity, per IEA data cited here.
insights.quote: The U.S. is starting a race with a significant handicap and their competitor is already dominant and running much faster.
insights.locator: China holds the largest share in global manufacturing

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's Yana Hryshko expects Chinese solar manufacturers will eventually leave the U.S. market entirely once continued operation there stops making financial sense, given the market's complexity, trade barriers, and regulatory burden.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Investors in U.S. solar assets should model a scenario of full Chinese manufacturer exit, not just reduced ownership stakes, as the medium-term base case.
insights.quote: I think eventually Chinese manufacturers will leave the U.S. market, because at some point, it will stop making financial sense.
insights.locator: I think eventually Chinese manufacturers will leave

insights.claim: SEMA Coalition's Yogin Kothari argues the earlier Chinese investment wave in the U.S. mainly built low-value final-assembly plants rather than higher-value cell/wafer fabs, meaning it extended rather than reduced U.S. dependence on Chinese solar imports.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
entities: Solar Energy Manufacturers for America (SEMA) Coalition
insights.so_what: Reframes the 2022-2023 Chinese investment boom as a dependency-deepening move rather than genuine reshoring, supporting the case for continued ownership restrictions.
insights.quote: When you take a step back, the investment from the Chinese and Chinese controlled entities in the United States is not actually to support a thriving U.S. domestic solar market.
insights.locator: When you take a step back, the investment

insights.claim: The same OBBB rules that limited Chinese investment also cut residential solar tax credits starting in 2026 and terminated other credits for solar facility construction begun after July 4, 2026, per Kate Logan of the Asia Society Policy Institute.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: U.S. clean-energy buildout is being slowed by the same legislation that targets Chinese investment, creating policy uncertainty on both the demand and supply side of the domestic solar market.
insights.quote: There's policy uncertainty more broadly toward clean energy installations. Then there is uncertainty for Chinese firms that are eager to invest in the U.S. market and have capital at the ready.
insights.locator: There's policy uncertainty more broadly toward clean energy

insights.claim: T1 Energy's Russell Gold says the policy shift incentivizes customers to buy from U.S. firms with transparent, China-free supply chains to avoid tariff and policy risk.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Supply-chain transparency is becoming a commercial selling point for U.S. solar suppliers, not just a compliance requirement.
insights.quote: If you know where your materials are coming from, you don't have to worry about tariffs. You don't have to worry about a change of policy.
insights.locator: If you know where your materials are coming from

insights.claim: Kothari argues the U.S. solar industry doesn't need to replicate China's excess production scale, only support domestic demand, since China's dominance reflects 25 years of deliberate industrial policy rather than any inherent technological advantage in a technology invented in the U.S.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
entities: Solar Energy Manufacturers for America (SEMA) Coalition
insights.so_what: Frames the U.S. reshoring goal as domestic self-sufficiency rather than global export competitiveness, a lower and more achievable bar for investors evaluating U.S. solar manufacturing plays.
insights.quote: China has made real industrial policy decisions over the last 25 years to take ownership of this technology that was invented in the United States. There's actually no inherent advantage that China has on solar that we can't do here if we want to.
insights.locator: China has made real industrial policy decisions
